title: Borrowing in Rust
content:
### Borrowing in Rust

Borrowing allows you to access a value without taking ownership. References are created with `&` and `&mut`.

---

### Borrowing Rules

| Rule | Description |
|------|-------------|
| One mutable reference XOR any number of immutable references | No data races |
| References must always be valid | No dangling references |
| The borrower's scope cannot outlive the owner | Ensures memory safety |

---

### Mutable vs Immutable Borrowing

```rust
fn main() {
    let mut s = String::from("hello");
    
    let r1 = &s;      // immutable borrow
    let r2 = &s;      // OK: multiple immutable borrows
    // let r3 = &mut s; // ERROR: cannot borrow as mutable while immutable exists
    println!("{}, {}", r1, r2);  // r1 and r2 go out of scope here
    
    let r3 = &mut s;  // OK: no immutable borrows active
    r3.push_str(" world");
    println!("{}", r3);
}
```

> **Key Point:** The borrow checker enforces that you cannot have a mutable reference and immutable references to the same data at the same time. This prevents data races at compile time. The scope of a reference ends at its last use.

quiz.question: How many mutable references can exist to a value at once?
